title: Advaita-Siddhi — Madhusūdana's defense of Advaita against Vyāsatīrtha through four mithyātva-lakṣaṇas
attacker_text: Advaita-Siddhi (c. 1570); supplementary Bhakti-Rasāyana and Vedānta-Kalpa-Latikā; defended downstream by Brahmānanda Sarasvatī's Laghu-Candrikā
target_position_summary: Vyāsatīrtha's Nyāyāmṛta — the formally-engineered Navya-Dvaita position that destructively criticises every available mithyātva-lakṣaṇa and positively establishes pañca-bheda through formal anumāna. Madhusūdana's reply is structurally a defensive operation: the doctrine is preserved by offering four non-equivalent lakṣaṇas of mithyātva, each crafted to evade a distinct Vyāsatīrtha objection, on the meta-thesis that the underlying property mithyātva is reachable by multiple independent characterisations none of which collapses mithyā into asat.

arguments.name: argument-1: lakṣaṇa-1 defended — sva-āśrayatva-niṣṭha-atyantābhāva-pratiyogitva
premises: Define mithyātva as: being the counter-positive of an absolute absence located in its own substrate.
When brahma-jñāna arises, the world (including its component items) is recognised as absent in its very substrate (Brahman).
Therefore the world is the counter-positive of an absence located in its substrate; therefore the world is mithyā.

Vyāsatīrtha's regress charge (that pratiyogitva itself must be real-or-mithyā) is met by noting that pratiyogitva is anirvacanīya: it is the relational property by which something is picked out as the counterpart of an absence, and such relational properties are not first-order entities requiring their own ontological grounding.
arguments.inference: The first lakṣaṇa survives the regress objection if anirvacanīya is admitted as a legitimate ontological category for relational properties.
arguments.conclusion: Mithyātva is formally definable; the lakṣaṇa-1 reaches the world correctly without catching items it should not catch (Brahman is not the counter-positive of any absence in its own substrate).

positions_actually_attacked.position: Nyāyāmṛta's argument that lakṣaṇa-1 generates regress on pratiyogitva.
Madhva's underlying Mithyātvānumāna-Khaṇḍana charge that the inferential apparatus for mithyātva is dialectically defective.

positions_left_intact.position: Jayatīrtha's earlier defense of pañca-bheda by sākṣi-pramāṇa rather than by formal anumāna is not directly engaged by the lakṣaṇa-1 defense; the disagreement on whether sākṣi-pramāṇa can override formal inference remains untouched.

later_responses.response_text: Pariṃala — argues that Madhusūdana's anirvacanīya-pratiyogitva escape is not principled because it generalises: any objectionable item could be saved by declaring its problematic feature anirvacanīya
later_responses.verdict: the criticism turns the anirvacanīya category into a universal solvent, against which Madhusūdana's school is held to need a principled restriction.

later_responses.response_text: Laghu-Candrikā — defends Madhusūdana by specifying that anirvacanīya applies only where bivalent characterisation can be shown to fail by a specific dialectical exhibition, not as a general solvent
later_responses.verdict: preserves the doctrine by tightening the conditions on the anirvacanīya category.

arguments.user_thesis_test: Substantive defense of a substantive doctrine. The reply is genuine and the residual disagreement (over the conditions on the anirvacanīya category) is itself substantive. Neither side dissolves.

arguments.name: argument-2: lakṣaṇa-3 defended — sad-vilakṣaṇatva read as the conjunction (not-sat, not-asat)
premises: Define mithyātva as: sad-asad-vilakṣaṇa — other than both real and unreal.
Vyāsatīrtha's bivalence-violation charge presupposes that sad-asad exhausts the ontological space.

But the rope-snake illusion gives a phenomenological case where the snake is neither sat (it isn't actually there) nor asat (it was experienced); the experiential evidence itself warrants the third category.
Hence the lakṣaṇa is not stipulative but warranted by the very phenomenology that motivates the inquiry into ignorance.
The category mithyā is therefore a real third option whose pramāṇa-warrant is anubhava (immediate experience) of sublation-without-prior-non-existence.
arguments.inference: Sad-asad-vilakṣaṇatva is a phenomenologically-warranted third ontological category, not a stipulation.
arguments.conclusion: The lakṣaṇa is dialectically legitimate; mithyā ≠ asat is preserved as a hard distinction.

positions_actually_attacked.position: Madhva's characteristic polemical conflation of mithyā with asat (the move the user's framework explicitly rejects).

positions_left_intact.position: Jayatīrtha's pramāṇa-theoretic argument that anirvacanīya cannot be a pramāṇa-grounded category remains a separate axis.

later_responses.response_text: Already in Nyāyāmṛta: argues that the rope-snake case does not establish a third ontological category — the snake-cognition is itself a case of asat-khyāti or anyathā-khyāti depending on theory of error
later_responses.verdict: the reply requires a particular theory of error (yathārtha-khyāti for Madhva) that makes the snake-cognition fully real-of-something-different, not real-of-the-snake. The dispute reduces to a disagreement about theory of error.

arguments.user_thesis_test: This is the locus where the user's mithyā ≠ asat constraint is most directly defended. Madhusūdana's defense is exactly the constraint the user's framework adopts. The disagreement here is genuinely substantive — over theory of error and over whether the rope-snake phenomenology warrants a third category. Madhva's polemic against this lakṣaṇa is one place where the user's framework and Madhva diverge sharply.

arguments.name: argument-3: lakṣaṇa-2 defended — sva-niṣṭha-atyantābhāva (an alternative substrate-formulation)
premises: An alternative formulation: mithyātva = bearing an absence locatable in itself (rather than in its substrate).
This formulation evades the substrate-relativity worry of lakṣaṇa-1.
It captures the intuition that what is mithyā is what is, in some sense, not what it appears to be — there is an absence within it of the very being it appears to have.

Vyāsatīrtha's objection that this collapses into asat is met by distinguishing two absences: (a) absence-of-the-property-of-being-the-substrate-of-being (which the world bears, hence mithyā) and (b) absolute non-existence (which the world does not bear, hence not asat).
arguments.inference: Lakṣaṇa-2 supplies an alternative routing to mithyātva that evades the regress-charge of lakṣaṇa-1.
arguments.conclusion: Mithyātva is reachable by multiple non-equivalent lakṣaṇas; this very multiplicity is dialectically protective.

positions_actually_attacked.position: The argument that any single lakṣaṇa fails.

positions_left_intact.position: Madhva's charge against the doctrine itself (rather than against any single lakṣaṇa) is not addressed by the multiplicity-defense.

later_responses.response_text: Nyāyāmṛta replies: multiple defective lakṣaṇas do not amount to one good lakṣaṇa; the burden of proof is to give one lakṣaṇa that survives examination, not several
later_responses.verdict: raises a methodological question about whether disjunctive characterisation of an ontological property is dialectically legitimate.

arguments.user_thesis_test: The disjunctive defense is methodologically novel; Vyāsatīrtha's reply that disjunctive characterisations are not principled is a real methodological objection. Substantive.

arguments.name: argument-4: lakṣaṇa-4 defended — bādha-pratiyogitva read as the cancelling-cognition criterion
premises: Lakṣaṇa-4: mithyātva = being the counter-positive of the cognition that arises with brahma-jñāna and cancels what is to be cancelled.
This is a soteriological-functional definition: mithyā is whatever brahma-jñāna sublates.

Vyāsatīrtha's circularity charge (mithyā = bādhya by brahma-jñāna; brahma-jñāna = cognition that bādhates mithyā) is met by distinguishing the sublation-relation from the property of being-sublatable: the latter is a dispositional property that can be characterised independently of any particular sublation-event.

The asat objection (that asat items are also bādhya, in some sense) is met by typologising: asat items are not bādhya because they were never present-as-cognised (they were never there to be cancelled); mithyā items are bādhya in the strong sense (they were cognitively-present, then cancelled).
The discrimination between mithyā and asat is therefore phenomenologically grounded in the difference between cancelled-after-having-been-cognised (mithyā) and never-cognised-at-all (asat).
arguments.inference: Lakṣaṇa-4 is non-circular if dispositional reading of bādhya is admitted, and discriminates mithyā from asat by phenomenological criterion.
arguments.conclusion: The phenomenological-functional lakṣaṇa is the fall-back defense: even if the more formal lakṣaṇas are contested, the experiential ground of mithyā is secured.

positions_actually_attacked.position: Both the circularity and the asat-confusion charges against lakṣaṇa-4.

positions_left_intact.position: Madhva's deeper structural objection — that brahma-jñāna does not arise on the Advaita scheme — is not engaged.

later_responses.response_text: Nyāyāmṛta extends the criticism to dispositional bādhya: dispositional properties require manifest occasions for their manifestation, and the Advaitin cannot specify the occasions without smuggling in the very brahma-jñāna whose possibility is the prior question
later_responses.verdict: pushes the dialectic to a meta-question: under what conditions are dispositional ontological properties legitimate?

arguments.user_thesis_test: Substantive. The phenomenological defense is a real philosophical resource and the residual question (legitimacy of dispositional ontological properties) is genuinely open.

user_thesis_summary: Madhusūdana's four-lakṣaṇa strategy is the most sophisticated defensive operation in classical Vedānta. The structural achievement is the methodological move of disjunctive characterisation: by offering four non-equivalent lakṣaṇas, Madhusūdana ensures that no single dialectical defeat collapses the doctrine. Against the user's perspectivism hypothesis, the Madhusūdana-Vyāsatīrtha exchange is the strongest evidence of substantive disagreement: both interlocutors operate in the same technical idiom (post-Gaṅgeśa Navya-Nyāya), share a common conception of what counts as a successful argument, and yet the disagreement persists across multiple sub-issues (anirvacanīya category; bivalence; theory of error; disjunctive lakṣaṇas; dispositional properties). The defense does, however, vindicate the user's mithyā ≠ asat constraint: lakṣaṇa-3 explicitly preserves this distinction, and the defense rests on it. So the polemic both supports the user's framework (mithyā ≠ asat is genuinely Advaitic) and undermines the user's perspectivism hypothesis (the residual disagreements with Dvaita are substantive, not register-effects). Verdict: substantive-with-defended-response. The polemic is engaged at the highest technical level and survives the engagement.
primary_source_loci: Advaita-Siddhi dvitīya-paricheda (extended defenses against further Vyāsatīrtha objections)
Bhakti-Rasāyana — Madhusūdana's integration of bhakti into the Advaita scheme as a parallel project

scholarly_apparatus.work: Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, vol. 11 (Advaita Vedānta from 800 to 1200)
scholarly_apparatus.position: Reconstructs the four lakṣaṇas with full Navya-Nyāya formality and traces the dialectical structure of each defense.

scholarly_apparatus.work: Bhakti-rasa for the Advaitin Renunciate: Madhusūdana Sarasvatī's Theory of Devotional Sentiment (Religion of the Heart, ed. M. Lath, 1986)
scholarly_apparatus.position: Documents the broader project: Madhusūdana defends Advaita doctrinally while also integrating bhakti-rasa as a parallel Advaita-compatible affective discipline.

scholarly_apparatus.work: Advaita Vedānta and Vaiṣṇavism: The Philosophy of Madhusūdana Sarasvatī (Routledge, 2006)
scholarly_apparatus.position: Reads the Advaita-Siddhi as a coordinated defensive-and-constructive project, not merely a reply to Vyāsatīrtha; the four lakṣaṇas are part of a positive Advaita-restatement under hostile examination.
